Claude Code transcript

14 prompts · 307 messages · 94 tool calls · 1 commits · 3 pages

#12026-07-04T06:24:43.114Z

Cameo is a systems engineering tool? can you make sense of these files without us having the software?

10 bash · 1 write

Yes — and good news: we can read almost everything without Cameo. .mdzip files are just zip archives, and the model inside is standard XMI (UML 2.5 / SysML 1.x XML). I've extracted both into the scratchpad and parsed them.

What these two files are

  • ANDES_model.mdzip (84 MB) — the full ANDES (ELT-HIRES) instrument systems model. The core is a single 149 MB XML file (com.nomagic.magicdraw.uml_model.model). The BINARY-* entries are mostly RTF blobs: rich-text element descriptions with embedded images.
  • AstroMBSE_Profile.mdzip (1 MB) — a custom SysML profile the model depends on. It defines the project's own stereotypes: Product, Function, AstroMBSE_Requirement, AstroMBSE_Interface_Requirement, Interface_End, Interface_Channel, Fibre, CIDL document types, etc.

What's in the model

It's a serious, full-lifecycle MBSE model. Top-level packages:

  • 0.Requirements — ~6,700 requirements total, dominated by ~10,600 interface-requirement stereotype applications. Includes imported ESO/ELT standards and ICDs (each as a versioned baseline, apparently via ReqIF import) plus the ANDES Technical Requirements Specification.
  • 1.Instrument Specification — objectives, capabilities, scenarios, contexts, life cycle.
  • 2.Functional Analysis — observation and calibration functions, functional signals (593 activities, object flows).
  • 3.STRUCTURE — the product breakdown, 2,568 Product elements.
  • 4.Interfaces — internal/external interfaces, ~2,300 proxy ports, ~1,000 item flows.
  • 5.Subsystems Models — Fiber Link, Front End.
  • 6.WBS, 7.CIDL — work breakdown and Configuration Item Data Lists per subsystem (FL, CU, AO, KS, US, RU, YS, RS, FE).
  • Analysis & Budgets — mass budget, cooling-power budget, RV budget.

There are 1,147 diagrams (requirement flow-downs per subsystem, ICD relations, WBS views, verification tables…).

What we can and can't do without the software

Extractable with scripts: requirement IDs and full text (they're plain attributes in the XML — I verified), the product/package hierarchy, traceability links (DeriveReqt, Allocate, Refine — ~2,400 derive relations), interfaces and connections, instance tables and budget values. Essentially any report or cross-check you'd want.

Not practical: rendering the diagrams — the layout data is Cameo-proprietary. We can see which diagrams exist and which elements they reference, but not draw them as they appear in the tool.

The extracted files are in the session scratchpad and I left an outline.py script there. What would you like to dig into — e.g. dump requirements to CSV, trace a budget, or map a subsystem's interfaces?

#22026-07-04T06:33:01.786Z

ok, nice! can you find the req that apply to the data reduction WP (part of SW subsystem) and visualize how the flow down from the top?

21 bash · 7 edit · 6 write · 1 skill
#32026-07-04T06:41:35.953Z

Approach this as the design lead at a small studio known for their versatility, giving every client a visual identity pitched at the treatment the task actually calls for. Make deliberate choices about palette, typography, and layout that are specific to this subject, and avoid templated designs.

Read the request first

Calibrate treatment, not whether to design. A doc deserves the same craft as a landing page — what changes is the treatment that craft is delivered in.

Many requests call for a more utilitarian treatment: a plan, a memo, a demo. Make it polished: include real typographic hierarchy, considered spacing, and a proper palette, but avoid over-designing. Most pages do not need a flashy, gigantic hero. Keep flourishes tasteful and limited.

Some requests call for an editorial treatment: a landing page, a game, an app or tool they'll keep or share.

When unsure: a well-composed page is never the wrong answer; an over-designed visual identity sometimes is.

Fundamentals below apply to everything. The editorial process after that runs only when the read above says so.

Fundamentals for every artifact

Honor what's already there Look for an existing design system first — CLAUDE.md, a tokens or theme file, existing component styles. When one exists, apply it; everything below fills gaps and never overrides. Precedence is always: the user's own words, then the project's existing system, then your choices.

Ground it in the subject. If the subject isn't already clear, pin it: one concrete subject, its audience, and the page's single job. The subject's own world — its materials, instruments, vernacular — is where distinctive choices come from. Build with real content throughout, never lorem.

Pair typefaces Typography carries the page even when the page isn't about typography. The Artifact CSP blocks font CDNs, so don't link a webfont URL and risk a silent fallback. Instead inline the face as a @font-face data URI. Keep running text near 65 characters wide; set a type scale and stay on it; give headings text-wrap: balance, body text room to breathe, and uppercase labels a touch of letter-spacing.

Choose neutrals, don't default to them. A pure mid-grey reads as unconsidered; a grey with a slight hue bias toward the page's accent reads as chosen. Pure white and near-black are fine grounds when they suit the subject — the point is that the neutral was picked, not inherited.

Let layout do the spacing. Lay out sibling groups with flex or grid and gap, not per-element margins that silently collapse or double. Wide content — tables, code, diagrams — gets overflow-x: auto on its own container so the page body never scrolls sideways. Reach for font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums wherever digits line up in columns.

Avoid AI-generated design AI-generated design currently clusters around a few looks: warm cream (#F4F1EA) with a serif display and terracotta accent; near-black with a lone acid-green or vermilion pop; broadsheet hairline rules with dense columns; a purple-to-blue gradient hero on white; Inter or Space Grotesk as the "safe" face; emoji as section markers; everything centered; rounded-lg everywhere; accent bar/rail on rounded cards. Where the user pins down a visual direction, follow it exactly — their words always win, including when they ask for one of these looks. Where nothing is specified, don't spend that freedom on one of these defaults.

Build cleanly Be cognizant of overlapping elements, cascade collisions, silent font fallbacks; visual bugs hide in the gap between source and output. Close every non-void element, double-quote attributes, give keyboard focus a visible state, respect prefers-reduced-motion. For generative or decorative graphics, reach for Canvas or WebGL rather than hand-authoring long SVG path data.

CSS rules When writing the CSS, watch your selector specificities. It is easy to generate classes that cancel each other out — a type-based selector like .section fighting an element-based one like .cta over padding and margins between sections. Structure the cascade so it doesn't silently undo your spacing.

Writing the copy Words are design material, not decoration. Write from the user's side of the screen — name things by what people recognize, not how the system is built (a person manages notifications, not webhook config). Active voice; a control says exactly what happens ("Publish", then a toast that says "Published"). Errors explain what went wrong and how to fix it — no apologies, no vagueness. Specific beats clever.

Structure is information Structural devices, numbering, eyebrows, dividers, labels, should encode something true about the content, not decorate it. Many generic designs use numbered markers (01 / 02 / 03), but that's only appropriate if the content actually is a sequence - like a real process or a typed timeline where order carries information the reader needs. Question if choices like numbered markers actually make sense before incorporating them.

When it's a UI, not a document A dashboard or tool is scanned and operated, not read top-to-bottom, so the craft shifts from typography to information design. Surface the summary before the detail; encode state in form as well as number — a pill, a chip, a severity stripe — so what needs attention reads at a glance. Semantic color (good / warning / critical) is separate from the accent hue and doesn't count as your accent. Give sparklines and charts the same care as type: an area fill, a faint grid, an emphasized endpoint. What's interactive should look interactive.

Process

Before writing code, sketch a short design plan — a compact token system with color, type, and layout: - Color: describe the palette as 4–6 named hex values. - Type: typefaces for 2+ roles — a characterful display face used with restraint, a complementary body face, and a utility face for captions or data if needed. - Layout: a layout concept in one or two sentences.

Then build, following the plan and deriving every color and type decision from it.

When the request is editorial

The stance shifts: the client has already rejected proposals that felt templated, and is paying for a distinctive point of view. Make opinionated calls, and take one real aesthetic risk where it serves the work.

Review the design plan against the subject before building: if any part of it reads like the generic default you would produce for any similar page, revise that part, and note what you changed and why. Only after you've confirmed the plan's uniqueness do you write the code, following the revised plan exactly.

Principles

  • The hero is a thesis: open with the most characteristic thing in the subject's world — headline, image, live demo, interactive moment.
  • Typography carries the personality of the page. Pair the display and body faces deliberately, not the same families you would reach for on any other project, and set a clear type scale with intentional weights, widths, and spacing. Make the type treatment itself a memorable part of the design, not a neutral delivery vehicle for the content.
  • Leverage motion deliberately. Think about where and if animation can serve the subject: a page-load sequence, a scroll-triggered reveal, hover micro-interactions, ambient atmosphere. An orchestrated moment usually lands harder than scattered effects; choose what the direction calls for. However, sometimes less is more, and extra animation contributes to the feeling that the design is AI-generated.
  • Match complexity to the vision. Maximalist directions need elaborate execution; minimal directions need precision in spacing, type, and detail. Elegance is executing the chosen vision well.
  • Spend your boldness in one place; keep everything around it quiet. If the accent fights the ground, shift it toward analogous or drop saturation rather than replacing it.
2 write

Approach this as the design lead at a small studio known for their versatility, giving every client a visual identity pitched at the treatment the task actually calls for. Make deliberate choices about palette, typography, and layout that are specific to this subject, and avoid templated designs.

Read the request first

Calibrate treatment, not whether to design. A doc deserves the same craft as a landing page — what changes is the treatment that craft is delivered in.

Many requests call for a more utilitarian treatment: a plan, a memo, a demo. Make it polished: include real typographic hierarchy, considered spacing, and a proper palette, but avoid over-designing. Most pages do not need a flashy, gigantic hero. Keep flourishes tasteful and limited.

Some requests call for an editorial treatment: a landing page, a game, an app or tool they'll keep or share.

When unsure: a well-composed page is never the wrong answer; an over-designed visual identity sometimes is.

Fundamentals below apply to everything. The editorial process after that runs only when the read above says so.

Fundamentals for every artifact

Honor what's already there Look for an existing design system first — CLAUDE.md, a tokens or theme file, existing component styles. When one exists, apply it; everything below fills gaps and never overrides. Precedence is always: the user's own words, then the project's existing system, then your choices.

Ground it in the subject. If the subject isn't already clear, pin it: one concrete subject, its audience, and the page's single job. The subject's own world — its materials, instruments, vernacular — is where distinctive choices come from. Build with real content throughout, never lorem.

Pair typefaces Typography carries the page even when the page isn't about typography. The Artifact CSP blocks font CDNs, so don't link a webfont URL and risk a silent fallback. Instead inline the face as a @font-face data URI. Keep running text near 65 characters wide; set a type scale and stay on it; give headings text-wrap: balance, body text room to breathe, and uppercase labels a touch of letter-spacing.

Choose neutrals, don't default to them. A pure mid-grey reads as unconsidered; a grey with a slight hue bias toward the page's accent reads as chosen. Pure white and near-black are fine grounds when they suit the subject — the point is that the neutral was picked, not inherited.

Let layout do the spacing. Lay out sibling groups with flex or grid and gap, not per-element margins that silently collapse or double. Wide content — tables, code, diagrams — gets overflow-x: auto on its own container so the page body never scrolls sideways. Reach for font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums wherever digits line up in columns.

Avoid AI-generated design AI-generated design currently clusters around a few looks: warm cream (#F4F1EA) with a serif display and terracotta accent; near-black with a lone acid-green or vermilion pop; broadsheet hairline rules with dense columns; a purple-to-blue gradient hero on white; Inter or Space Grotesk as the "safe" face; emoji as section markers; everything centered; rounded-lg everywhere; accent bar/rail on rounded cards. Where the user pins down a visual direction, follow it exactly — their words always win, including when they ask for one of these looks. Where nothing is specified, don't spend that freedom on one of these defaults.

Build cleanly Be cognizant of overlapping elements, cascade collisions, silent font fallbacks; visual bugs hide in the gap between source and output. Close every non-void element, double-quote attributes, give keyboard focus a visible state, respect prefers-reduced-motion. For generative or decorative graphics, reach for Canvas or WebGL rather than hand-authoring long SVG path data.

CSS rules When writing the CSS, watch your selector specificities. It is easy to generate classes that cancel each other out — a type-based selector like .section fighting an element-based one like .cta over padding and margins between sections. Structure the cascade so it doesn't silently undo your spacing.

Writing the copy Words are design material, not decoration. Write from the user's side of the screen — name things by what people recognize, not how the system is built (a person manages notifications, not webhook config). Active voice; a control says exactly what happens ("Publish", then a toast that says "Published"). Errors explain what went wrong and how to fix it — no apologies, no vagueness. Specific beats clever.

Structure is information Structural devices, numbering, eyebrows, dividers, labels, should encode something true about the content, not decorate it. Many generic designs use numbered markers (01 / 02 / 03), but that's only appropriate if the content actually is a sequence - like a real process or a typed timeline where order carries information the reader needs. Question if choices like numbered markers actually make sense before incorporating them.

When it's a UI, not a document A dashboard or tool is scanned and operated, not read top-to-bottom, so the craft shifts from typography to information design. Surface the summary before the detail; encode state in form as well as number — a pill, a chip, a severity stripe — so what needs attention reads at a glance. Semantic color (good / warning / critical) is separate from the accent hue and doesn't count as your accent. Give sparklines and charts the same care as type: an area fill, a faint grid, an emphasized endpoint. What's interactive should look interactive.

Process

Before writing code, sketch a short design plan — a compact token system with color, type, and layout: - Color: describe the palette as 4–6 named hex values. - Type: typefaces for 2+ roles — a characterful display face used with restraint, a complementary body face, and a utility face for captions or data if needed. - Layout: a layout concept in one or two sentences.

Then build, following the plan and deriving every color and type decision from it.

When the request is editorial

The stance shifts: the client has already rejected proposals that felt templated, and is paying for a distinctive point of view. Make opinionated calls, and take one real aesthetic risk where it serves the work.

Review the design plan against the subject before building: if any part of it reads like the generic default you would produce for any similar page, revise that part, and note what you changed and why. Only after you've confirmed the plan's uniqueness do you write the code, following the revised plan exactly.

Principles

  • The hero is a thesis: open with the most characteristic thing in the subject's world — headline, image, live demo, interactive moment.
  • Typography carries the personality of the page. Pair the display and body faces deliberately, not the same families you would reach for on any other project, and set a clear type scale with intentional weights, widths, and spacing. Make the type treatment itself a memorable part of the design, not a neutral delivery vehicle for the content.
  • Leverage motion deliberately. Think about where and if animation can serve the subject: a page-load sequence, a scroll-triggered reveal, hover micro-interactions, ambient atmosphere. An orchestrated moment usually lands harder than scattered effects; choose what the direction calls for. However, sometimes less is more, and extra animation contributes to the feeling that the design is AI-generated.
  • Match complexity to the vision. Maximalist directions need elaborate execution; minimal directions need precision in spacing, type, and detail. Elegance is executing the chosen vision well.
  • Spend your boldness in one place; keep everything around it quiet. If the accent fights the ground, shift it toward analogous or drop saturation rather than replacing it.
#42026-07-04T09:42:21.444Z

continue pls

6 bash · 5 read · 2 edit · 1 write · 1 artifact

Done — the flow-down visualization is live: https://claude.ai/code/artifact/59108c49-d09b-426a-bd3c-2eb304bf23f6

What I found

The SW DRS work package (in 6.WBS / ANDES_WP / SW Sys Eng) has 14 requirements linked to it, coming from two source documents:

ANDES Technical Requirements Specification (8 requirements) - Seven flow-down chains, each three levels deep: top-level instrument requirement → SW-level derivation → DRS-level requirement. For example: [R-AND-65] "two observing modes" → [R-AND-65].3 "SW shall support both modes" → [R-AND-65].3.SW2 "DRS shall reduce seeing-limited and IFU data". The chains cover observing modes, wavelength range extension (U/K band), spectral resolution preservation (±5%), calibration validity (no night-time calibrations), sky subtraction (<1%), interpolation (FWHM growth <20%), and ELT data-flow compliance. - [R-AND-120] (the DRS section of the TRS) applies directly with no intermediate derivation.

Common Requirements for ELT Instruments (6 requirements) - [R-INS-896] (data reduction package) flows down to .1 and branches into three DRS leaves: quality-control monitoring in the reduction cascade, interactive science-grade reduction, and CPL/HDRL/Reflex compliance. - [R-INS-928], [R-INS-1049], [R-INS-1050] (Phase 3 / Science Data Product standards) apply directly.

Two honest caveats, shown in the diagram

  • One link, [R-AND-88].1 → [R-AND-88].1.SW1, has no modeled DeriveReqt — I inferred it from the naming convention only (drawn dashed, labeled "implied"). That may be a gap worth fixing in the model.
  • The [R-INS-896] flow-downs aren't linked by DeriveReqt either; the model expresses derivation there by nesting the child requirements inside the parent (drawn dotted).

All requirement texts in the diagram are quoted verbatim from the XMI. The extraction scripts (index_model.py, trace_flowdown.py) are in the scratchpad, so we can rerun this for any other work package — the same WP-dependency pattern is used throughout the WBS.

#52026-07-04T09:54:44.757Z

cool thx! what do you think of this, from your general knowledge of SE ? good flowdown? what is missing? will a DRS that fulfills these reqs produce the expected results?

1 write · 1 bash

I dug a bit further in the model to ground this, and the answer to your last question is essentially no — and the model itself contains the evidence. Here's my assessment.

Is it a good flow-down, methodologically?

Yes, above average for what I see in practice. It's a real three-tier derivation (document requirement → SW-level → DRS-level) rather than a flat allocation; the leaves are quantified and verifiable (±5% resolution, 1% sky subtraction, 20% FWHM growth, 24 h calibration validity); source documents are kept as versioned baselines with stable IDs; and compliance status fields are maintained. The hygiene issues are minor but real: the [R-AND-88].1 → .SW1 link exists only by naming convention, the R-INS-896 branch uses containment while the TRS branch uses DeriveReqt (two mechanisms for the same semantics makes automated queries fragile), a couple of derivations are verbatim echoes of their parent ([R-AND-115].1 and .1.SW1 are identical — a derivation should add an architectural decision, not copy), and the texts could use a review pass ("degradate", "recored", "shall be fully compliance").

What's missing — and this is the interesting part

The radial-velocity thread never reaches the DRS work package. This is the flagship performance of the instrument, and the model knows it: the Analysis & Budgets / RV Budget package contains a Total RV Error Budget with an explicit "Reduction Pipeline RV Error" contributor under "Software Algorythms RV Error". But no requirement linked to SW DRS carries any RV or wavelength-accuracy allocation. Even more telling: [R-SYS-4].0a reads "The DRS shall provide a correction to the wavelength solution along the full slit by using spectra of the simultaneous reference…" — a DRS requirement by its own text — yet it's linked to the SW Sys Eng WP, not SW DRS. Similarly [R-AND-84] (wavelength precision better than 1 m/s RMS) and [R-AND-85] (calibration stability) flow to "SW Architect - SWSE" and the Optical Architect, then stop. The budget-to-requirement-to-WP thread exists in pieces but isn't closed.

Beyond RV, things I'd expect in a DRS spec that aren't among the 14:

  • Error propagation — per-pixel variance and quality flags on all products (a science-grade pipeline must-have; HDRL encourages but doesn't enforce it)
  • SNR / extraction efficiency — nothing bounds how much signal the reduction may lose relative to the photon limit
  • Flux/continuum fidelity — critical for transmission spectroscopy; only indirectly referenced via [R-AND-112]
  • Telluric correction — conspicuously absent for an instrument whose science lives in the red/NIR
  • Throughput/latency — no "reduce faster than acquisition" or QC-latency requirement, though [R-INS-896].1.SW1 implies night-time QC
  • Verification: none of the 14 has a Verify or Satisfy link. The model does do this elsewhere — the wavelength requirements link to "Wavelength precision – L1…L4" test cases — so the practice exists, it just hasn't been applied to the DRS set yet.

One currency flag: [R-INS-896].1.SW3 mandates Reflex, which ESO has been superseding with EDPS as the pipeline execution environment. That's inherited from the Common Requirements baseline and worth checking against ESO's current expectations.

Would a compliant DRS produce the expected results?

Fulfilling these 14 gives you an ESO-ecosystem-compliant pipeline (CPL/HDRL, Phase 3 products, DFS keywords) that doesn't smear the resolution, handles both modes, and subtracts sky to 1%. That's necessary but not sufficient. A contractor could satisfy every one of them and still deliver a DRS that misses the science, because nothing in this set binds the pipeline to the RV error budget, wavelength-solution accuracy, error propagation, or telluric handling. My guess is the real depth lives in the referenced E-AND-SW-SPE-* documents outside the model, so this is likely an incomplete linkage rather than an incomplete project — but as modeled, the flow-down is a compliance skeleton, not an acceptance basis. If I were doing the review, my top action items would be: allocate a quantified number from "Reduction Pipeline RV Error" into a DRS requirement, re-home [R-SYS-4].0a to the DRS WP, and add Verify links for the 14.

#62026-07-04T10:06:28.066Z

indeed, that mirrors my thinking. pls save the artifact and a drs-flowdown.md with this assessment into the cwd

5 write · 3 bash

All set. The project directory now contains everything from this session:

  • drs-flowdown.html and drs-flowdown.md — the diagram and the assessment
  • tools/ — the five scripts: outline.py (package/diagram overview), index_model.py (builds the pickle index the others read), trace_flowdown.py (WP requirement tracing — WP id is hardcoded near the top, swap it for other work packages), gap_check.py (the RV-gap and Verify-link queries), gen_viz.py (diagram row generator)
  • CLAUDE.md — documents the mdzip format, the model's non-obvious conventions (the four requirement stereotypes, plain-dependency WP allocation, the three flow-down mechanisms), and the tool workflow from unzip through visualization

I verified the tools run via uv run from the project root — they carry PEP 723 headers so no project setup is needed. Note the extracted XMI and model_index.pkl live in the session scratchpad and aren't copied (150 MB+, regenerable in a couple of minutes with steps 1 and 3 in CLAUDE.md).

#72026-07-04T10:11:56.068Z

nice, thx! pls init a git repo, add the mdzip to gitignore then commit everything else. then use gh to madke a public repo andes-drs-reqs and push to it

4 bash · 1 write

Done — the repo is live at https://github.com/ivh/andes-drs-reqs.

What was set up:

  • Git repo initialized with main as the default branch, one root commit (5be5c48) containing the assessment (drs-flowdown.md), the diagram (drs-flowdown.html), the five tools/ scripts, and CLAUDE.md.
  • .gitignore excludes *.mdzip as requested, and I also added the regenerable intermediates the CLAUDE.md workflow produces (extracted/, model_index.pkl, flowdown.json, rows_fragment.html) so future runs don't dirty the repo — easy to trim if you'd rather track any of those.
  • Created as public under your ivh account and pushed; main is tracking origin/main.

One thing to be aware of since the repo is public: drs-flowdown.md and the HTML quote the requirement texts verbatim, including from ESO-published documents ([R-INS-], [R-AND-]). If any of that is consortium-internal, you may want to flip the repo to private (gh repo edit ivh/andes-drs-reqs --visibility private).

#82026-07-04T10:14:55.216Z

github does not render html files that are in th repo?

#92026-07-04T10:15:37.371Z

github does not render html files that are in th repo? or rather insists on showing the source instread of letting the browser render it

4 bash · 1 toolsearch · 1 monitor

Yes — that's intentional on GitHub's part: the repo file viewer always shows source, and raw.githubusercontent.com serves files as plain text specifically so browsers won't execute HTML from repos (an XSS protection). There's no setting to change that for repo files.

The standard solution is GitHub Pages, which I've just enabled for the repo (serving the main branch root). Once the first build finishes — typically a minute or two — the diagram will render at:

https://ivh.github.io/andes-drs-reqs/drs-flowdown.html

I've set a watch on the URL and will confirm as soon as it returns 200. Since Pages serves the branch tip, the page also auto-updates on every push, so it stays in sync with the repo — no separate publishing step needed.

Background command "Wait for Pages build and check the page responds" completed (exit code 0)

1 read

b16er6hky toolu_01NdDFv43ikgQSJ7hEHFJ5sG /private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-tom-ANDES-cameo/9e0bd2d8-e804-49c4-9209-7e6d629adac1/tasks/b16er6hky.output completed

Background command "Check Pages URL again after build delay" completed (exit code 0)

1 read
#122026-07-04T10:17:21.354Z

rerun, github hiccup

2 bash · 1 monitor

Rebuild triggered — the previous build had been stuck in "building" with no error, so a hiccup on their end is plausible. A fresh build is queued and the new watch reports on either outcome: "built" (plus the actual HTTP status of the diagram URL) or the build error message if it fails again. I'll report as soon as it lands.

#132026-07-04T10:18:20.324Z

bsd7bee04

Monitor event: "GitHub Pages deploy of andes-drs-reqs"

Pages live: HTTP 200

1 toolsearch · 1 taskstop · 1 bash

The diagram is live and rendering properly: https://ivh.github.io/andes-drs-reqs/drs-flowdown.html

The rebuild completed ("built", HTTP 200) and the page is served as text/html, so browsers render it instead of showing source. Both watches have finished and are cleaned up.

Since Pages serves the main branch root, any future push updates the site automatically — and other files are reachable the same way (e.g. https://ivh.github.io/andes-drs-reqs/drs-flowdown.md, though Pages serves markdown as plain text; the rendered view of that one is better on GitHub itself).

#142026-07-04T10:19:20.373Z

nice. pls add a very short REAME.md that mentions what all this is about and links to the page and the md file with the assessment

1 write · 1 bash

README added and pushed. It's intentionally brief: one sentence on what the repo is (DRS requirements extracted from the Cameo SysML model without the software), links to the rendered flow-down diagram on Pages and to drs-flowdown.md with the assessment, and a pointer to tools/ + CLAUDE.md for rerunning the analysis, with a note that the .mdzip files aren't included. It's live on the repo front page at https://github.com/ivh/andes-drs-reqs.

599429c2026-07-04T10:19:40.561Z

User2026-07-04T06:24:43.114Z

Cameo is a systems engineering tool? can you make sense of these files without us having the software?

Assistant2026-07-04T06:24:48.713Z
Thinking
Assistant2026-07-04T06:24:50.130Z

Yes — Cameo Systems Modeler (No Magic / Dassault Systèmes) is a SysML/UML modeling tool. Its project files are usually zip archives containing XMI (XML), so there's a good chance we can dig into them with standard tools. Let me look at what you have.

Assistant2026-07-04T06:24:50.456Z
$ Bash
List files in the cameo directory
ls -laR /Users/tom/ANDES/cameo | head -100
Tool reply2026-07-04T06:24:50.899Z
/Users/tom/ANDES/cameo:
total 83616
drwxr-xr-x  4 tom staff      128 Jul  4 08:24 .

Assistant2026-07-04T06:25:53.145Z
📝 Write outline.py
/private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-tom-ANDES-cameo/9e0bd2d8-e804-49c4-9209-7e6d629adac1/scratchpad/outline.py
# /// script
# requires-python = ">=3.11"
# dependencies = ["lxml"]
# ///
"""Outline the package tree and diagrams of a MagicDraw/Cameo XMI model."""
import sys
from lxml import etree

path = sys.argv[1]
max_depth = int(sys.argv[2]) if len(sys.argv) > 2 else 3

UML = "{http://www.omg.org/spec/UML/20131001}"
XMI = "{http://www.omg.org/spec/XMI/20131001}"

pkg_stack = []
diagrams = []

def emit(name, depth, kind):
    print("  " * depth + f"- {name}" + (f"  [{kind}]" if kind else ""))

for event, elem in etree.iterparse(path, events=("start", "end"), huge_tree=True):
    tag = elem.tag
    if event == "start" and tag == "packagedElement":
        t = elem.get(XMI + "type", "")
        if t == "uml:Package":
            name = elem.get("name", "(unnamed)")
            if len(pkg_stack) < max_depth:
                emit(name, len(pkg_stack), "")
            pkg_stack.append(name)
    elif event == "end":
        if tag == "packagedElement" and elem.get(XMI + "type") == "uml:Package":
            pkg_stack.pop()
        if elem.get(XMI + "type") == "uml:Diagram":
            diagrams.append(elem.get("name", "(unnamed)"))
        # free memory
        if tag == "packagedElement":
            elem.clear()

print(f"\n=== {len(diagrams)} diagrams (first 40) ===")
for d in diagrams[:40]:
    print("  ", d)
Tool reply2026-07-04T06:25:53.195Z

cd "$SCRATCH" && uv run outline.py model/com.nomagic.magicdraw.uml_model.model 2 2>&1 | head -80

grep -o "<sysml:Requirement [^>]*>" "$SCRATCH/model/com.nomagic.magicdraw.uml_model.model" | grep "Text='" | head -3

<sysml:Requirement xmi:id='Baseline_4159824256c050ad-0000dce4-1000006-obj2_' base_Class='Baseline_4159824256c050ad-0000dce4-1000006-obj2' Id='2' Text='&lt;html&gt;&lt;font style=font-weight:bold&gt;Standard_Components_and_Guidelines_for_Cooling_Cir...&lt;/font&gt;&lt;span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;font style=font-weight:bold&gt;Embedded Object&lt;/font&gt;&lt;span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/html&gt;'/>
<sysml:Requirement xmi:id='Baseline_4159824256c050ad-0000dce4-1000006-obj6_' base_Class='Baseline_4159824256c050ad-0000dce4-1000006-obj6' Id='6' Text='The purpose is to provide sufficient reliability in the operation of those systems by the use of tested components and to improve the maintainability.'/>
<sysml:Requirement xmi:id='Baseline_4159824256c050ad-0000dce4-1000006-obj5_' base_Class='Baseline_4159824256c050ad-0000dce4-1000006-obj5' Id='5' Text='This document defines standard components and workmanship principles for the construction of chilled medium cooling systems to be installed on ESO telescopes, for example as part of an astronomical instrument.'/>
Assistant2026-07-04T06:26:47.030Z
Thinking
Assistant2026-07-04T06:26:47.034Z

Yes — and good news: we can read almost everything without Cameo. .mdzip files are just zip archives, and the model inside is standard XMI (UML 2.5 / SysML 1.x XML). I've extracted both into the scratchpad and parsed them.

What these two files are

  • ANDES_model.mdzip (84 MB) — the full ANDES (ELT-HIRES) instrument systems model. The core is a single 149 MB XML file (com.nomagic.magicdraw.uml_model.model). The BINARY-* entries are mostly RTF blobs: rich-text element descriptions with embedded images.
  • AstroMBSE_Profile.mdzip (1 MB) — a custom SysML profile the model depends on. It defines the project's own stereotypes: Product, Function, AstroMBSE_Requirement, AstroMBSE_Interface_Requirement, Interface_End, Interface_Channel, Fibre, CIDL document types, etc.

What's in the model

It's a serious, full-lifecycle MBSE model. Top-level packages:

  • 0.Requirements — ~6,700 requirements total, dominated by ~10,600 interface-requirement stereotype applications. Includes imported ESO/ELT standards and ICDs (each as a versioned baseline, apparently via ReqIF import) plus the ANDES Technical Requirements Specification.
  • 1.Instrument Specification — objectives, capabilities, scenarios, contexts, life cycle.
  • 2.Functional Analysis — observation and calibration functions, functional signals (593 activities, object flows).
  • 3.STRUCTURE — the product breakdown, 2,568 Product elements.
  • 4.Interfaces — internal/external interfaces, ~2,300 proxy ports, ~1,000 item flows.
  • 5.Subsystems Models — Fiber Link, Front End.
  • 6.WBS, 7.CIDL — work breakdown and Configuration Item Data Lists per subsystem (FL, CU, AO, KS, US, RU, YS, RS, FE).
  • Analysis & Budgets — mass budget, cooling-power budget, RV budget.

There are 1,147 diagrams (requirement flow-downs per subsystem, ICD relations, WBS views, verification tables…).

What we can and can't do without the software

Extractable with scripts: requirement IDs and full text (they're plain attributes in the XML — I verified), the product/package hierarchy, traceability links (DeriveReqt, Allocate, Refine — ~2,400 derive relations), interfaces and connections, instance tables and budget values. Essentially any report or cross-check you'd want.

Not practical: rendering the diagrams — the layout data is Cameo-proprietary. We can see which diagrams exist and which elements they reference, but not draw them as they appear in the tool.

The extracted files are in the session scratchpad and I left an outline.py script there. What would you like to dig into — e.g. dump requirements to CSV, trace a budget, or map a subsystem's interfaces?

Assistant2026-07-04T06:34:29.921Z
📝 Write index_model.py
/private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-tom-ANDES-cameo/9e0bd2d8-e804-49c4-9209-7e6d629adac1/scratchpad/index_model.py
# /// script
# requires-python = ">=3.11"
# dependencies = ["lxml"]
# ///
"""Index a MagicDraw/Cameo XMI model: elements, requirements, and
dependency-type relationships. Writes a pickle for downstream queries."""
import pickle
import sys
from lxml import etree

path = sys.argv[1]
out = sys.argv[2]

XMI = "{http://www.omg.org/spec/XMI/20131001}"

elements = {}        # id -> dict(name, type, parent)
deps = {}            # dep_id -> dict(clients=[], suppliers=[])
requirements = {}    # base_Class id -> dict(id_attr, text, stereo)
stereo_of_dep = {}   # dep/abstraction id -> stereotype name (DeriveReqt, Allocate, ...)
other_stereos = {}   # base element id -> [stereotype names]

DEP_TYPES = {"uml:Dependency", "uml:Abstraction", "uml:Realization", "uml:Usage"}
REQ_STEREOS = {
    "Requirement", "AstroMBSE_Requirement", "AstroMBSE_Interface_Requirement",
    "Test_Requirement",
}

stack = []  # element ids (or None) for parent tracking
cur_dep = []

for event, elem in etree.iterparse(path, events=("start", "end"), huge_tree=True):
    tag = elem.tag
    if event == "start":
        eid = elem.get(XMI + "id")
        etype = elem.get(XMI + "type", "")
        if eid and (etype or elem.get("name")):
            elements[eid] = {
                "name": elem.get("name", ""),
                "type": etype or tag,
                "parent": next((s for s in reversed(stack) if s), None),
            }
        stack.append(eid)
        if etype in DEP_TYPES and eid:
            deps[eid] = {"clients": [], "suppliers": [], "type": etype}
            # client/supplier may be space-separated attrs
            for attr, key in (("client", "clients"), ("supplier", "suppliers")):
                v = elem.get(attr)
                if v:
                    deps[eid][key].extend(v.split())
            cur_dep.append(eid)
        elif cur_dep and tag in ("client", "supplier"):
            ref = elem.get(XMI + "idref") or elem.get("href", "").split("#")[-1]
            if ref:
                deps[cur_dep[-1]][tag + "s"].append(ref)
    else:
        eid = stack.pop()
        etype = elem.get(XMI + "type", "")
        if etype in DEP_TYPES and cur_dep and cur_dep[-1] == eid:
            cur_dep.pop()
        # stereotype applications live at root level, tags namespaced by profile
        if tag.startswith("{http://www.omg.org/spec/SysML") or "magicdraw.com" in tag or "spec/Customization" in tag:
            local = tag.split("}")[1]
            base = None
            for k, v in elem.attrib.items():
                if k.startswith("base_"):
                    base = v
                    break
            if base:
                if local in REQ_STEREOS:
                    requirements[base] = {
                        "rid": elem.get("Id", ""),
                        "text": elem.get("Text", ""),
                        "stereo": local,
                    }
                elif base in deps or local in ("DeriveReqt", "Allocate", "Refine",
                                               "Satisfy", "Verify", "associatedElmnt",
                                               "Defines", "Trace"):
                    stereo_of_dep[base] = local
                else:
                    other_stereos.setdefault(base, []).append(local)
        if tag == "packagedElement":
            elem.clear()

with open(out, "wb") as f:
    pickle.dump({"elements": elements, "deps": deps, "requirements": requirements,
                 "stereo_of_dep": stereo_of_dep, "other_stereos": other_stereos}, f)

print(f"elements: {len(elements)}")
print(f"deps: {len(deps)}")
print(f"requirements: {len(requirements)}")
print(f"stereotyped deps: {len(stereo_of_dep)}")
from collections import Counter
print(Counter(stereo_of_dep.values()).most_common(15))
Tool reply2026-07-04T06:34:29.957Z

cd "$SCRATCH" && uv run index_model.py model/com.nomagic.magicdraw.uml_model.model model_index.pkl

📝 Write explore_dr.py
/private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-tom-ANDES-cameo/9e0bd2d8-e804-49c4-9209-7e6d629adac1/scratchpad/explore_dr.py
# /// script
# requires-python = ">=3.11"
# dependencies = []
# ///
"""Explore data-reduction elements: parent chains and attached requirements."""
import pickle

with open("model_index.pkl", "rb") as f:
    ix = pickle.load(f)
E, D, R, S = ix["elements"], ix["deps"], ix["requirements"], ix["stereo_of_dep"]

def chain(eid):
    parts = []
    while eid:
        e = E.get(eid)
        if not e:
            break
        if e["name"]:
            parts.append(e["name"])
        eid = e["parent"]
    return " < ".join(parts)

# incoming/outgoing dep maps
by_client, by_supplier = {}, {}
for did, d in D.items():
    for c in d["clients"]:
        by_client.setdefault(c, []).append(did)
    for s in d["suppliers"]:
        by_supplier.setdefault(s, []).append(did)

def show(eid):
    e = E[eid]
    print(f"\n=== {e['name']} ({e['type']}) id={eid}")
    print(f"    path: {chain(eid)}")
    for label, m, other_key in (("as client", by_client, "suppliers"),
                                ("as supplier", by_supplier, "clients")):
        dids = m.get(eid, [])
        print(f"    {label}: {len(dids)} deps")
        from collections import Counter
        cnt = Counter(tuple(S.get(did, ["plain"])) for did in dids)
        for k, v in cnt.most_common():
            print(f"       {v}x {k}")

candidates = [eid for eid, e in E.items()
              if e["name"] in ("SW DRS", "Data Reduction Software",
                               "Data Reduction", "sw.data Reduction Software")
              and e["type"] in ("uml:Class", "uml:InstanceSpecification")]
for c in candidates:
    show(c)

/private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-tom-ANDES-cameo/9e0bd2d8-e804-49c4-9209-7e6d629adac1/scratchpad/explore_dr2.py
# /// script
# requires-python = ">=3.11"
# dependencies = []
# ///
"""Show what SW DRS work package links to."""
import pickle

with open("model_index.pkl", "rb") as f:
    ix = pickle.load(f)
E, D, R, S = ix["elements"], ix["deps"], ix["requirements"], ix["stereo_of_dep"]

def chain(eid):
    parts = []
    while eid:
        e = E.get(eid)
        if not e:
            break
        if e["name"]:
            parts.append(e["name"])
        eid = e["parent"]
    return " < ".join(parts)

WP = "_2022x_1_ef20353_1686237423990_559313_159"  # SW DRS
for did, d in D.items():
    if WP in d["clients"]:
        for s in d["suppliers"]:
            e = E.get(s, {})
            r = R.get(s)
            tag = f"REQ {r['rid']} [{r['stereo']}]" if r else e.get("type", "?")
            print(f"  -> {e.get('name','?')} ({tag})")
            print(f"       {chain(s)}")

📝 Write trace_flowdown.py
/private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-tom-ANDES-cameo/9e0bd2d8-e804-49c4-9209-7e6d629adac1/scratchpad/trace_flowdown.py
# /// script
# requires-python = ">=3.11"
# dependencies = []
# ///
"""Trace DeriveReqt chains upward from the SW DRS work-package requirements
and dump the flow-down DAG as JSON."""
import json
import pickle

with open("model_index.pkl", "rb") as f:
    ix = pickle.load(f)
E, D, R, S = ix["elements"], ix["deps"], ix["requirements"], ix["stereo_of_dep"]

def chain(eid):
    parts = []
    while eid:
        e = E.get(eid)
        if not e:
            break
        if e["name"]:
            parts.append(e["name"])
        eid = e["parent"]
    return parts

# Requirement info can be attached to a different copy (baseline vs working);
# fall back to name matching.
req_by_name = {}
for base, r in R.items():
    e = E.get(base)
    if e and e["name"]:
        req_by_name.setdefault(e["name"], r)

def req_info(eid):
    if eid in R:
        return R[eid]
    e = E.get(eid)
    if e and e["name"] in req_by_name:
        return req_by_name[e["name"]]
    return None

# derive edges: client (derived) -> suppliers (sources). Upward = client to supplier.
UP_STEREOS = {"DeriveReqt"}
up = {}    # derived id -> [(source id, stereo)]
down = {}  # source id -> [(derived id, stereo)]
for did, stereos in S.items():
    if not UP_STEREOS.intersection(stereos):
        continue
    d = D.get(did)
    if not d:
        continue
    for c in d["clients"]:
        for s in d["suppliers"]:
            up.setdefault(c, []).append(s)
            down.setdefault(s, []).append(c)

WP = "_2022x_1_ef20353_1686237423990_559313_159"  # SW DRS
wp_reqs = []
for did, d in D.items():
    if WP in d["clients"]:
        wp_reqs.extend(d["suppliers"])

nodes, edges = {}, set()

def add_node(eid):
    if eid in nodes:
        return
    e = E.get(eid, {})
    r = req_info(eid)
    path = chain(E.get(eid, {}).get("parent"))
    doc = path[-2] if len(path) >= 2 else ""  # package under 0.Requirements
    nodes[eid] = {
        "id": eid,
        "name": e.get("name", "?"),
        "text": (r or {}).get("text", ""),
        "stereo": (r or {}).get("stereo", ""),
        "doc": doc,
        "path": " < ".join(path),
    }

def walk_up(eid, depth=0):
    add_node(eid)
    if depth > 12:
        return
    for src in up.get(eid, []):
        if (src, eid) not in edges:
            edges.add((src, eid))
            walk_up(src, depth + 1)

for rid in wp_reqs:
    walk_up(rid)

print(f"WP reqs: {len(wp_reqs)}, nodes: {len(nodes)}, edges: {len(edges)}")
for rid in wp_reqs:
    n = nodes[rid]
    ups = [nodes[s]["name"] for s in up.get(rid, [])]
    print(f"  {n['name']}: up -> {ups}")
    print(f"      doc: {n['doc']}")
    txt = n["text"].replace("\n", " ")
    print(f"      text: {txt[:140]}")

with open("flowdown.json", "w") as f:
    json.dump({
        "wp_reqs": wp_reqs,
        "nodes": list(nodes.values()),
        "edges": [{"from": a, "to": b} for a, b in edges],
    }, f, indent=1)

# roots
roots = [n for eid, n in nodes.items() if not up.get(eid)]
print("\nRoots (no further upward derivation):")
for n in roots:
    print(f"  {n['name']}  [{n['doc']}]")

/private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-tom-ANDES-cameo/9e0bd2d8-e804-49c4-9209-7e6d629adac1/scratchpad/debug_missing.py
# /// script
# requires-python = ">=3.11"
# dependencies = []
# ///
"""Debug missing derive links and requirement texts."""
import pickle

with open("model_index.pkl", "rb") as f:
    ix = pickle.load(f)
E, D, R, S = ix["elements"], ix["deps"], ix["requirements"], ix["stereo_of_dep"]

def chain(eid):
    parts = []
    while eid:
        e = E.get(eid)
        if not e:
            break
        parts.append(e["name"] or f"<{e['type']}>")
        eid = e["parent"]
    return " < ".join(parts)

by_end = {}
for did, d in D.items():
    for c in d["clients"]:
        by_end.setdefault(c, []).append((did, "client"))
    for s in d["suppliers"]:
        by_end.setdefault(s, []).append((did, "supplier"))

for name in ("[R-AND-88].1.SW1", "[R-AND-88].1", "[R-INS-1049]", "[R-INS-896].1.SW1"):
    print(f"\n### elements named {name}:")
    for eid, e in E.items():
        if e["name"] == name:
            print(f"  id={eid} type={e['type']}")
            print(f"    path: {chain(e['parent'])}")
            print(f"    in R: {eid in R}", R.get(eid, {}).get("rid", ""))
            for did, role in by_end.get(eid, [])[:8]:
                d = D[did]
                other = d["suppliers"] if role == "client" else d["clients"]
                onames = [E.get(o, {}).get("name", o) for o in other]
                print(f"    dep[{','.join(S.get(did, ['plain']))}] as {role} -> {onames}")

Assistant2026-07-04T06:39:24.679Z
📝 Write trace_flowdown.py
/private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-tom-ANDES-cameo/9e0bd2d8-e804-49c4-9209-7e6d629adac1/scratchpad/trace_flowdown.py
# /// script
# requires-python = ">=3.11"
# dependencies = []
# ///
"""Trace requirement flow-down upward from the SW DRS work-package requirements.
Upward edges: DeriveReqt deps, containment nesting, and naming convention
([X].1.SW1 -> [X].1 -> [X]). Dumps DAG as JSON."""
import json
import pickle
import re

with open("model_index.pkl", "rb") as f:
    ix = pickle.load(f)
E, D, R, S = ix["elements"], ix["deps"], ix["requirements"], ix["stereo_of_dep"]

def chain_list(eid):
    parts = []
    while eid:
        e = E.get(eid)
        if not e:
            break
        parts.append(e["name"] or "")
        eid = e["parent"]
    return parts

def doc_of(eid):
    parts = chain_list(eid)
    for i, p in enumerate(parts):
        if p == "0.Requirements" and i >= 1:
            return parts[i - 1]
    return parts[-2] if len(parts) >= 2 else ""

req_ids = set(R)
name_to_ids = {}
for eid, e in E.items():
    if e["name"]:
        name_to_ids.setdefault(e["name"], []).append(eid)

# --- upward edge collection ---
up = {}  # derived id -> list of (source id, kind)

for did, stereos in S.items():
    if "DeriveReqt" not in stereos:
        continue
    d = D.get(did)
    if not d:
        continue
    for c in d["clients"]:
        for s in d["suppliers"]:
            up.setdefault(c, []).append((s, "derive"))

def nesting_parent(eid):
    p = E.get(eid, {}).get("parent")
    return p if p in req_ids else None

def naming_parent(eid):
    name = E.get(eid, {}).get("name", "")
    m = re.match(r"^(.*)\.[A-Za-z0-9]+$", name)
    if not m:
        return None
    parent_name = m.group(1)
    cands = [i for i in name_to_ids.get(parent_name, []) if i in req_ids]
    return cands[0] if cands else None

WP = "_2022x_1_ef20353_1686237423990_559313_159"  # SW DRS
wp_reqs = []
for did, d in D.items():
    if WP in d["clients"]:
        wp_reqs.extend(d["suppliers"])

nodes, edges = {}, []
edge_seen = set()

def add_node(eid):
    if eid in nodes:
        return
    e = E.get(eid, {})
    r = R.get(eid, {})
    nodes[eid] = {
        "id": eid,
        "name": e.get("name", "?"),
        "text": r.get("text", ""),
        "stereo": r.get("stereo", ""),
        "doc": doc_of(eid),
    }

def add_edge(src, dst, kind):
    if (src, dst) in edge_seen:
        return False
    edge_seen.add((src, dst))
    edges.append({"from": src, "to": dst, "kind": kind})
    return True

def walk_up(eid, depth=0):
    add_node(eid)
    if depth > 12:
        return
    ups = list(up.get(eid, []))
    if not ups:
        np_ = nesting_parent(eid)
        if np_:
            ups = [(np_, "nested")]
        else:
            nm = naming_parent(eid)
            if nm:
                ups = [(nm, "naming")]
    for src, kind in ups:
        if add_edge(src, eid, kind):
            walk_up(src, depth + 1)

for rid in wp_reqs:
    walk_up(rid)

roots = [eid for eid in nodes if not any(e["to"] == eid for e in edges)
         or all(e["to"] != eid for e in edges)]
roots = [eid for eid in nodes if not [e for e in edges if e["to"] == eid and e["from"] != eid]]
# root = never a 'to' target... wait, edges go source->derived, so root = node that is
# never a 'to'? No: root is a node with no upward edge, i.e. never appears as 'to'.
roots = [eid for eid in nodes if not [e for e in edges if e["to"] == eid]]
roots = [eid for eid in nodes if not [e for e in edges if e["from"] != eid and e["to"] != eid]]

# simpler: node with no incoming 'up' recorded = no edge where it is the derived end
derived_ends = {e["to"] for e in edges}
roots = [eid for eid in nodes if eid not in derived_ends and eid not in
         {e2["to"] for e2 in edges}]
roots = [eid for eid in nodes if eid not in derived_ends]
# but roots must be ones REACHED, i.e. sources with no own upward edge
sources = {e["from"] for e in edges}
top = [eid for eid in nodes if eid in sources and not [e for e in edges if e["to"] == eid]]
isolated = [eid for eid in wp_reqs if eid not in derived_ends and eid not in sources]

print(f"WP reqs: {len(wp_reqs)}, nodes: {len(nodes)}, edges: {len(edges)}")
print("\nTop-level sources:")
for eid in top:
    n = nodes[eid]
    print(f"  {n['name']}  [{n['doc']}]")
    # any other deps from these roots?
    for did, d in D.items():
        if eid in d["clients"]:
            st = ",".join(S.get(did, ["plain"]))
            for s in d["suppliers"]:
                print(f"     root as client --{st}--> {E.get(s, {}).get('name', s)} [{doc_of(s)}]")
print("\nIsolated WP reqs (no upward link found):")
for eid in isolated:
    print(f"  {nodes[eid]['name']}")

with open("flowdown.json", "w") as f:
    json.dump({"wp_reqs": wp_reqs, "nodes": list(nodes.values()), "edges": edges}, f, indent=1)

# root = never a 'to' target... wait, edges go source->derived, so root = node that is
# never a 'to'? No: root is a node with no upward edge, i.e. never appears as 'to'.
roots = [eid for eid in nodes if not [e for e in edges if e["to"] == eid]]
roots = [eid for eid in nodes if not [e for e in edges if e["from"] != eid and e["to"] != eid]]

# simpler: node with no incoming 'up' recorded = no edge where it is the derived end

roots = [eid for eid in nodes if eid not in derived_ends and eid not in
         {e2["to"] for e2 in edges}]
roots = [eid for eid in nodes if eid not in derived_ends]
# but roots must be ones REACHED, i.e. sources with no own upward edge
sources = {e["from"] for e in edges}

sources = {e["from"] for e in edges}

def nesting_parent(eid):
    p = E.get(eid, {}).get("parent")
    return p if p in req_ids else None
+
def is_real_req(eid):
    # section headings are requirement-stereotyped too; real reqs have [R-...] names
    return eid in req_ids and E.get(eid, {}).get("name", "").startswith("[")

def nesting_parent(eid):
    p = E.get(eid, {}).get("parent")
    return p if p and is_real_req(p) else None

Assistant2026-07-04T06:40:50.399Z
$ Bash
Dump flow-down DAG edges and node texts
SCRATCH=/private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-tom-ANDES-cameo/9e0bd2d8-e804-49c4-9209-7e6d629adac1/scratchpad
cd "$SCRATCH" && uv run --with rich python -c "
import json
d = json.load(open('flowdown.json'))
nodes = {n['id']: n for n in d['nodes']}
print('EDGES:')
for e in d['edges']:
    print(f\"  {nodes[e['from']]['name']} --{e['kind']}--> {nodes[e['to']]['name']}\")
print()
for n in d['nodes']:
    t = n['text'].replace(chr(10),' ')
    print(f\"{n['name']} [{n['doc'][:40]}] ({n['stereo']})\")
    print(f\"   {t[:180]}\")
"

[R-AND-65].3.SW2 [* ANDES Technical Requirements Specifica] (AstroMBSE_Requirement)
   The ANDES DRS software shall be able to reduce data for seeing-limited mode data and IFU mode data
[R-AND-65].3 [* ANDES Technical Requirements Specifica] (AstroMBSE_Requirement)
   The ANDES Software shall provide support to observing mode in Seeing limited and IFU mode configuration
[R-AND-65] [* ANDES Technical Requirements Specifica] (AstroMBSE_Requirement)
   The instrument shall have two observing modes: 1. Seeing-limited mode   2. IFU mode

   The DRS shall cover reduction procedure in the full wavelength range of ANDES and shall support the extension to U band and K band. The DRS shall be modular to tolerate gaps and fu
[R-AND-70].1 [* ANDES Technical Requirements Specifica] (AstroMBSE_Requirement)
   The ANDES SW shall be able to support extension of U-band and K-band and tolerate gaps and wavelength extensions.

   The baseline design of ANDES shall cover the wavelength range specified in [R-AND-94]. As a goal, the wavelength range will be extended to U-band and/or K-band.
[R-AND-112].1.SW1 [* ANDES Technical Requirements Specifica] (AstroMBSE_Requirement)
   The ANDES DRS shall not require night-time calibrations to reduce the data and fullfil the requirements on accuracy, precisions, flux calibration and resolution. All day-time calib

   The ANDES SW shall not require night-time calibrations to reduce the data and fullfil the requirements on accuracy, precisions, flux calibration and resolution. All day-time calibr
[R-AND-112] [* ANDES Technical Requirements Specifica] (AstroMBSE_Requirement)
   Validity period of calibrations Except for standard stars, and scientific observations with simultaneous calibrations, night-time calibrations shall not be needed to fulfil the per
[R-AND-114].1.SW1 [* ANDES Technical Requirements Specifica] (AstroMBSE_Requirement)
   For observations in which optimal sky subtraction is requested (i.e. one sub-FoV is serving as a simultaneous sky probe), the sky subtraction obtained by the DRS shall be better th
[R-AND-114].1 [* ANDES Technical Requirements Specifica] (AstroMBSE_Requirement)
   For observations in which optimal sky subtraction is requested (i.e. one sub-FoV is serving as a simultaneous sky probe), the sky subtraction shall be better than 1% of the measure
[R-AND-114] [* ANDES Technical Requirements Specifica] (AstroMBSE_Requirement)
   Sky subtraction For observations in which optimal sky subtraction is requested (i.e. one sub-FoV is serving as a simultaneous sky probe), the sky subtraction shall be better than 1
[R-AND-115].1.SW1 [* ANDES Technical Requirements Specifica] (AstroMBSE_Requirement)
   The ANDES Data Reduction, when interpolating onto a regular output grid, shall not increase the FWHM of a Nyquist sampled Gaussian by more than 20%.
[R-AND-115].1 [* ANDES Technical Requirements Specifica] (AstroMBSE_Requirement)
   The ANDES Data Reduction, when interpolating onto a regular output grid, shall not increase the FWHM of a Nyquist sampled Gaussian by more than 20%.
[R-AND-115] [* ANDES Technical Requirements Specifica] (AstroMBSE_Requirement)
   Interpolation When interpolating onto a regular output grid, the data pipeline shall not increase the FWHM of a Nyquist sampled Gaussian by more than 20%.
[R-AND-119].1.SW1 [* ANDES Technical Requirements Specifica] (AstroMBSE_Requirement)
   DRS data product shall be fully compliance with the ELT data flow system as specified in AD-8
[R-AND-119].1 [* ANDES Technical Requirements Specifica] (AstroMBSE_Requirement)
   The ANDES software shall be fully integrated in the ELT data flow system. Deliverables are specified and described in AD8.
[R-AND-119] [* ANDES Technical Requirements Specifica] (AstroMBSE_Requirement)
   ANDES shall be fully integrated in the ELT data flow system. Deliverables are specified and described in AD8.
[R-AND-120] [* ANDES Technical Requirements Specifica] (AstroMBSE_Requirement)
   Data Reduction Software (DRS) The Pipeline shall be compliant with AD8.  The data reduction pipeline shall provide science grade data products for both seeing-limited and IFU modes
[R-INS-896].1.SW1 [* Common Requirements for ELT Instrument] (AstroMBSE_Requirement_Local)
   The DRS shall provide algorithms in the reduction cascade to monitor basic quality control to be stored in the FITS header that can be processed during the night for quality contro

   The ANDES data reduction package shall support the real-time basic quality control, science-grade pipeline processing, and interactive off-line data reduction, as specified in #898
[R-INS-896] [* Common Requirements for ELT Instrument] (AstroMBSE_Requirement_Local)
   A data reduction package supporting the real-time basic quality control, science-grade pipeline processing, and interactive off-line data reduction, as specified in #898, #1049 and

Tool reply2026-07-04T06:41:35.953Z
Launching skill: artifact-design
User2026-07-04T06:41:35.953Z

Approach this as the design lead at a small studio known for their versatility, giving every client a visual identity pitched at the treatment the task actually calls for. Make deliberate choices about palette, typography, and layout that are specific to this subject, and avoid templated designs.

Read the request first

Calibrate treatment, not whether to design. A doc deserves the same craft as a landing page — what changes is the treatment that craft is delivered in.

Many requests call for a more utilitarian treatment: a plan, a memo, a demo. Make it polished: include real typographic hierarchy, considered spacing, and a proper palette, but avoid over-designing. Most pages do not need a flashy, gigantic hero. Keep flourishes tasteful and limited.

Some requests call for an editorial treatment: a landing page, a game, an app or tool they'll keep or share.

When unsure: a well-composed page is never the wrong answer; an over-designed visual identity sometimes is.

Fundamentals below apply to everything. The editorial process after that runs only when the read above says so.

Fundamentals for every artifact

Honor what's already there Look for an existing design system first — CLAUDE.md, a tokens or theme file, existing component styles. When one exists, apply it; everything below fills gaps and never overrides. Precedence is always: the user's own words, then the project's existing system, then your choices.

Ground it in the subject. If the subject isn't already clear, pin it: one concrete subject, its audience, and the page's single job. The subject's own world — its materials, instruments, vernacular — is where distinctive choices come from. Build with real content throughout, never lorem.

Pair typefaces Typography carries the page even when the page isn't about typography. The Artifact CSP blocks font CDNs, so don't link a webfont URL and risk a silent fallback. Instead inline the face as a @font-face data URI. Keep running text near 65 characters wide; set a type scale and stay on it; give headings text-wrap: balance, body text room to breathe, and uppercase labels a touch of letter-spacing.

Choose neutrals, don't default to them. A pure mid-grey reads as unconsidered; a grey with a slight hue bias toward the page's accent reads as chosen. Pure white and near-black are fine grounds when they suit the subject — the point is that the neutral was picked, not inherited.

Let layout do the spacing. Lay out sibling groups with flex or grid and gap, not per-element margins that silently collapse or double. Wide content — tables, code, diagrams — gets overflow-x: auto on its own container so the page body never scrolls sideways. Reach for font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums wherever digits line up in columns.

Avoid AI-generated design AI-generated design currently clusters around a few looks: warm cream (#F4F1EA) with a serif display and terracotta accent; near-black with a lone acid-green or vermilion pop; broadsheet hairline rules with dense columns; a purple-to-blue gradient hero on white; Inter or Space Grotesk as the "safe" face; emoji as section markers; everything centered; rounded-lg everywhere; accent bar/rail on rounded cards. Where the user pins down a visual direction, follow it exactly — their words always win, including when they ask for one of these looks. Where nothing is specified, don't spend that freedom on one of these defaults.

Build cleanly Be cognizant of overlapping elements, cascade collisions, silent font fallbacks; visual bugs hide in the gap between source and output. Close every non-void element, double-quote attributes, give keyboard focus a visible state, respect prefers-reduced-motion. For generative or decorative graphics, reach for Canvas or WebGL rather than hand-authoring long SVG path data.

CSS rules When writing the CSS, watch your selector specificities. It is easy to generate classes that cancel each other out — a type-based selector like .section fighting an element-based one like .cta over padding and margins between sections. Structure the cascade so it doesn't silently undo your spacing.

Writing the copy Words are design material, not decoration. Write from the user's side of the screen — name things by what people recognize, not how the system is built (a person manages notifications, not webhook config). Active voice; a control says exactly what happens ("Publish", then a toast that says "Published"). Errors explain what went wrong and how to fix it — no apologies, no vagueness. Specific beats clever.

Structure is information Structural devices, numbering, eyebrows, dividers, labels, should encode something true about the content, not decorate it. Many generic designs use numbered markers (01 / 02 / 03), but that's only appropriate if the content actually is a sequence - like a real process or a typed timeline where order carries information the reader needs. Question if choices like numbered markers actually make sense before incorporating them.

When it's a UI, not a document A dashboard or tool is scanned and operated, not read top-to-bottom, so the craft shifts from typography to information design. Surface the summary before the detail; encode state in form as well as number — a pill, a chip, a severity stripe — so what needs attention reads at a glance. Semantic color (good / warning / critical) is separate from the accent hue and doesn't count as your accent. Give sparklines and charts the same care as type: an area fill, a faint grid, an emphasized endpoint. What's interactive should look interactive.

Process

Before writing code, sketch a short design plan — a compact token system with color, type, and layout: - Color: describe the palette as 4–6 named hex values. - Type: typefaces for 2+ roles — a characterful display face used with restraint, a complementary body face, and a utility face for captions or data if needed. - Layout: a layout concept in one or two sentences.

Then build, following the plan and deriving every color and type decision from it.

When the request is editorial

The stance shifts: the client has already rejected proposals that felt templated, and is paying for a distinctive point of view. Make opinionated calls, and take one real aesthetic risk where it serves the work.

Review the design plan against the subject before building: if any part of it reads like the generic default you would produce for any similar page, revise that part, and note what you changed and why. Only after you've confirmed the plan's uniqueness do you write the code, following the revised plan exactly.

Principles

  • The hero is a thesis: open with the most characteristic thing in the subject's world — headline, image, live demo, interactive moment.
  • Typography carries the personality of the page. Pair the display and body faces deliberately, not the same families you would reach for on any other project, and set a clear type scale with intentional weights, widths, and spacing. Make the type treatment itself a memorable part of the design, not a neutral delivery vehicle for the content.
  • Leverage motion deliberately. Think about where and if animation can serve the subject: a page-load sequence, a scroll-triggered reveal, hover micro-interactions, ambient atmosphere. An orchestrated moment usually lands harder than scattered effects; choose what the direction calls for. However, sometimes less is more, and extra animation contributes to the feeling that the design is AI-generated.
  • Match complexity to the vision. Maximalist directions need elaborate execution; minimal directions need precision in spacing, type, and detail. Elegance is executing the chosen vision well.
  • Spend your boldness in one place; keep everything around it quiet. If the accent fights the ground, shift it toward analogous or drop saturation rather than replacing it.

/private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-tom-ANDES-cameo/9e0bd2d8-e804-49c4-9209-7e6d629adac1/scratchpad/gen_viz.py
# /// script
# requires-python = ">=3.11"
# dependencies = []
# ///
"""Generate the flow-down diagram HTML from flowdown.json."""
import html
import json

with open("flowdown.json") as f:
    data = json.load(f)
N = {n["name"]: n for n in data["nodes"]}

def card(name, doc_cls, extra_note=""):
    n = N[name]
    txt = html.escape(n["text"]).replace("&#10;", " ").replace("\n", " ")
    note = f'<span class="note">{extra_note}</span>' if extra_note else ""
    return (f'<div class="card {doc_cls}"><span class="rid">{html.escape(name)}</span>'
            f'{note}<p>{txt}</p></div>')

# row layout: (L0, edge1, L1, edge2, L2) ; edge kinds: derive|nested|naming|direct
trs_chains = [
    ("[R-AND-65]", "derive", "[R-AND-65].3", "derive", "[R-AND-65].3.SW2"),
    ("[R-AND-70]", "derive", "[R-AND-70].1", "derive", "[R-AND-70].1.SW1"),
    ("[R-AND-88]", "derive", "[R-AND-88].1", "naming", "[R-AND-88].1.SW1"),
    ("[R-AND-112]", "derive", "[R-AND-112].1", "derive", "[R-AND-112].1.SW1"),
    ("[R-AND-114]", "derive", "[R-AND-114].1", "derive", "[R-AND-114].1.SW1"),
    ("[R-AND-115]", "derive", "[R-AND-115].1", "derive", "[R-AND-115].1.SW1"),
    ("[R-AND-119]", "derive", "[R-AND-119].1", "derive", "[R-AND-119].1.SW1"),
]
trs_direct = ["[R-AND-120]"]
ins_direct = ["[R-INS-928]", "[R-INS-1049]", "[R-INS-1050]"]

EDGE_CLS = {"derive": "solid", "nested": "dotted", "naming": "dashed"}
EDGE_LBL = {"derive": "", "nested": "", "naming": "implied"}

rows = []

def conn(kind):
    lbl = f'<em>{EDGE_LBL[kind]}</em>' if EDGE_LBL[kind] else ""
    return f'<div class="conn {EDGE_CLS[kind]}">{lbl}</div>'

def chain_row(l0, e1, l1, e2, l2, cls):
    rows.append(
        card(l0, cls) + conn(e1) + card(l1, cls) + conn(e2) + card(l2, cls)
        + '<div class="conn solid stub"></div>'
    )

def direct_row(l0, cls):
    rows.append(
        card(l0, cls)
        + '<div class="conn solid direct"><em>applies directly</em></div>'
    )

def header_row(text):
    rows.append(f'<div class="ghead">{text}</div>')

header_row("ANDES Technical Requirements Specification &mdash; ESO-published, flow-down by ANDES architects")
for c in trs_chains:
    extra = ""
    chain_row(*c, "trs")
direct_row("[R-AND-120]", "trs")

header_row("Common Requirements for ELT Instruments &mdash; ESO standard, local flow-down")
# R-INS-896 subtree: L0 and L1 span 3 rows, three leaves branch off
rows.append(
    '<div class="card ins span3">' + card_inner("[R-INS-896]") if False else ""
)
rows.pop()

def card_span(name, doc_cls, span):
    n = N[name]
    txt = html.escape(n["text"]).replace("&#10;", " ").replace("\n", " ")
    return (f'<div class="card {doc_cls} span{span}"><span class="rid">{html.escape(name)}</span>'
            f'<p>{txt}</p></div>')

leaves = ["[R-INS-896].1.SW1", "[R-INS-896].1.SW2", "[R-INS-896].1.SW3"]
tree = (
    card_span("[R-INS-896]", "ins", 3)
    + '<div class="conn solid span3"></div>'
    + card_span("[R-INS-896].1", "ins", 3)
    + '<div class="conn dotted branch btop"><i class="v"></i></div>'
    + card(leaves[0], "ins")
    + '<div class="conn solid stub"></div>'
    + '<div class="conn dotted branch bmid"><i class="v"></i></div>'
    + card(leaves[1], "ins")
    + '<div class="conn solid stub"></div>'
    + '<div class="conn dotted branch bbot"><i class="v"></i></div>'
    + card(leaves[2], "ins")
    + '<div class="conn solid stub"></div>'
)
rows.append(tree)
for name in ins_direct:
    direct_row(name, "ins")

body_rows = "\n".join(rows)
with open("rows_fragment.html", "w") as f:
    f.write(body_rows)
print("rows written, chars:", len(body_rows))
# also dump full texts for eyeballing
for n in data["nodes"]:
    print("---", n["name"])
    print(n["text"])

/private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-tom-ANDES-cameo/9e0bd2d8-e804-49c4-9209-7e6d629adac1/scratchpad/gen_viz.py
# /// script
# requires-python = ">=3.11"
# dependencies = []
# ///
"""Generate the flow-down diagram grid cells from flowdown.json."""
import html
import json

with open("flowdown.json") as f:
    data = json.load(f)
N = {n["name"]: n for n in data["nodes"]}

NOTES = {"[R-AND-112]": "also linked to SCAO work packages"}

def card(name, doc_cls, span=0):
    n = N[name]
    txt = html.escape(n["text"]).replace("\n", " ")
    note = f'<span class="note">{NOTES[name]}</span>' if name in NOTES else ""
    cls = f"card {doc_cls}" + (f" span{span}" if span else "")
    return (f'<div class="{cls}"><span class="rid">{html.escape(name)}</span>'
            f'<p>{txt}</p>{note}</div>')

EDGE_CLS = {"derive": "solid", "naming": "dashed"}

def conn(kind):
    lbl = '<em>implied</em>' if kind == "naming" else ""
    return f'<div class="conn {EDGE_CLS[kind]}">{lbl}</div>'

trs_chains = [
    ("[R-AND-65]", "derive", "[R-AND-65].3", "derive", "[R-AND-65].3.SW2"),
    ("[R-AND-70]", "derive", "[R-AND-70].1", "derive", "[R-AND-70].1.SW1"),
    ("[R-AND-88]", "derive", "[R-AND-88].1", "naming", "[R-AND-88].1.SW1"),
    ("[R-AND-112]", "derive", "[R-AND-112].1", "derive", "[R-AND-112].1.SW1"),
    ("[R-AND-114]", "derive", "[R-AND-114].1", "derive", "[R-AND-114].1.SW1"),
    ("[R-AND-115]", "derive", "[R-AND-115].1", "derive", "[R-AND-115].1.SW1"),
    ("[R-AND-119]", "derive", "[R-AND-119].1", "derive", "[R-AND-119].1.SW1"),
]

rows = []
rows.append('<div class="lvl c1">Document requirement</div>'
            '<div class="lvl c3">SW-level flow-down</div>'
            '<div class="lvl c5">DRS-level requirement</div>'
            '<div class="lvl c7">Work package</div>')

rows.append('<div class="ghead">ANDES Technical Requirements Specification'
            ' &mdash; ESO-published, architect flow-down</div>')
for l0, e1, l1, e2, l2 in trs_chains:
    rows.append(card(l0, "trs") + conn(e1) + card(l1, "trs") + conn(e2)
                + card(l2, "trs") + '<div class="conn solid stub"></div>')
rows.append(card("[R-AND-120]", "trs")
            + '<div class="conn solid direct"><em>applies directly</em></div>')

rows.append('<div class="ghead">Common Requirements for ELT Instruments'
            ' &mdash; ESO standard, local flow-down</div>')
tree = (card("[R-INS-896]", "ins", 3) + '<div class="conn solid span3"></div>'
        + card("[R-INS-896].1", "ins", 3))
for i, leaf in enumerate(["[R-INS-896].1.SW1", "[R-INS-896].1.SW2", "[R-INS-896].1.SW3"]):
    pos = ["btop", "bmid", "bbot"][i]
    tree += (f'<div class="conn dotted branch {pos}"><i class="v"></i><i class="ah"></i></div>'
             + card(leaf, "ins") + '<div class="conn solid stub"></div>')
rows.append(tree)
for name in ("[R-INS-928]", "[R-INS-1049]", "[R-INS-1050]"):
    rows.append(card(name, "ins")
                + '<div class="conn solid direct"><em>applies directly</em></div>')

with open("rows_fragment.html", "w") as f:
    f.write("\n".join(rows))
print("rows written")
